Epilogue: `leave` (= `mov rsp, rbp; pop rbp` — restores caller's RBP and RSP). 6. `ret`: pops return address into RIP (RSP += 8), execution continues in caller. Frame is destroyed — RSP back to pre-call value.

questions.question: Explain the difference between the Microsoft x64 and System V AMD64 calling conventions. Cover: argument registers, shadow space vs red zone, and stack alignment requirements.
questions.explanation: Argument registers: Microsoft uses RCX, RDX, R8, R9 (first 4 args, rest on stack). System V uses RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9 (first 6 args in registers). Shadow space: Microsoft reserves 32 bytes below return address for register spill (always present). Red zone: System V provides 128 bytes below RSP usable by leaf functions without adjustment (Microsoft has no red zone — any use below RSP risks corruption by interrupts). Stack alignment: both require RSP multiple of 16 at `call`. Return value: both use RAX.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a process and a thread?
options: A process has its own virtual address space; threads share the same address space within a process
questions.correctAnswer: A process has its own virtual address space; threads share the same address space within a process
questions.explanation: A process has its own MMU page tables (rooted at CR3), file descriptors, and credentials. Threads within a process share the same address space, file descriptors, and heap, but each has its own stack and register context. This sharing is why threads are lighter-weight but also why they need synchronization.

questions.question: What is the approximate cost of a context switch between threads in the same process?
questions.correctAnswer: ~1-5 microseconds
questions.explanation: Same-process context switch: save current thread's GPRs + optionally FPU state (XSAVE), scheduler selects next thread, restore its register state, IRETQ. Direct cost: 1-5 µs. Cross-process switch adds CR3 reload (TLB flush, mitigated by PCID) → 3-10 µs. Indirect cost: cache warmup of 5-100 µs.

questions.question: Why does `std::thread::hardware_concurrency()` not give linear speedup for CPU-bound work?
options: SMT threads share execution units and memory bandwidth — only 15-30% throughput improvement per logical thread
The OS limits the number of active threadsaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltalt
questions.correctAnswer: SMT threads share execution units and memory bandwidth — only 15-30% throughput improvement per logical thread
questions.explanation: `hardware_concurrency()` returns the number of logical cores (e.g., 16 on 8-core with HT). SMT (Hyper-Threading) duplicates architectural state but shares execution units (ALUs, FPUs) and caches. Two threads on the same physical core compete for these resources, yielding only 15-30% combined improvement. Plus, the OS has other threads that also need CPU time.

questions.question: What is CPU affinity and why is it used in game engines?
options: A security feature to prevent cheatingaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltalt (standard)
Constraining threads to specific cores to preserve cache warmth — avoids cache-cold migrations
A way to combine multiple CPUs into onealtaltaltaltaltaltaltaltalt . — as specified
questions.correctAnswer: Constraining threads to specific cores to preserve cache warmth — avoids cache-cold migrations
questions.explanation: CPU affinity (`sched_setaffinity`/`SetThreadAffinityMask`) pins a thread to specific cores. When the scheduler migrates a thread, its working set is cold (not in the new core's L1/L2 cache). Pinning latency-critical threads (audio, render) preserves cache warmth and avoids the 5-100 µs cache reload penalty.

questions.question: What is a thread pool and why is it used?
options: A collection of idle threads waiting for work — amortizes thread creation costs (~10-50 µs) by reusing workers
questions.correctAnswer: A collection of idle threads waiting for work — amortizes thread creation costs (~10-50 µs) by reusing workers
questions.explanation: Thread creation involves stack allocation, TCB initialization, and scheduler registration (~10-50 µs). A thread pool keeps long-lived workers ready, distributing tasks via work-stealing deques (lock-free Chase-Lev or bounded MPMC). This avoids the creation/destruction overhead for every small task.

questions.question: What is the danger of a real-time priority thread (SCHED_FIFO / THREAD_PRIORITY_TIME_CRITICAL)?
options: If it never yields, it can freeze the entire system — the scheduler cannot preempt it for lower-priority threads
questions.correctAnswer: If it never yields, it can freeze the entire system — the scheduler cannot preempt it for lower-priority threads
questions.explanation: Real-time threads have fixed priorities and immediately preempt all lower-priority threads. A SCHED_FIFO thread that enters an infinite loop will never be preempted — the desktop, mouse cursor, terminal, and even kernel helper threads may starve. Game engines use real-time priority for audio/render threads but with extreme care.

questions.question: What is the lock contention trap with fine-grained locking?
options: Many threads contending for a brief critical section spend most time blocked and context-switching — single-threaded performance can be faster
The scheduler ignores locked threadsaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltalt on x86 .
questions.correctAnswer: Many threads contending for a brief critical section spend most time blocked and context-switching — single-threaded performance can be faster
questions.explanation: If 8 threads contend for a mutex protecting a 1 ns increment: 7 threads are blocked at any moment, each lock handoff requires a context switch, and actual throughput may be ~20% of single-thread performance. Solutions: atomics for simple counters, partition data so threads don't contend, or batch work to minimize critical section frequency.

questions.question: How does the Linux CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler) work?
options: Fixed time slices for all threadsaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltalt
Uses vruntime-weighted red-black tree — the thread with the lowest vruntime runs next
Round-robin with fixed prioritiesaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltalt . — as specified
questions.correctAnswer: Uses vruntime-weighted red-black tree — the thread with the lowest vruntime runs next
questions.explanation: CFS maintains a red-black tree ordered by vruntime (virtual runtime — actual runtime weighted by priority). The leftmost node (lowest vruntime) runs next. Target latency: 6 ms period (all runnable threads get a slice within this window). Minimum granularity: 0.75 ms (prevents excessive context switching with many threads).

questions.question: What are green threads (user-space threads / fibers)?
options: Threads multiplexed onto OS threads cooperatively — switching costs ~10-30 ns with no kernel involvement
questions.correctAnswer: Threads multiplexed onto OS threads cooperatively — switching costs ~10-30 ns with no kernel involvement
questions.explanation: Green threads switch cooperatively via stack pointer swap + callee-saved register save/restore — just ~10-30 ns vs 1-5 µs for kernel context switch. The tradeoff: if one green thread blocks on I/O, ALL green threads on that OS thread are stuck (head-of-line blocking). They require non-blocking I/O (epoll, IOCP) to be practical.

questions.question: What does the `task_struct` (Linux) or `ETHREAD` (Windows) contain for a thread?
options: Saved register context (GPRs, RIP, RSP, RFLAGS, FS/GS base, FPU/SIMD state), stack pointers, scheduling info
questions.correctAnswer: Saved register context (GPRs, RIP, RSP, RFLAGS, FS/GS base, FPU/SIMD state), stack pointers, scheduling info
questions.explanation: The thread control block stores everything needed to suspend and resume execution: general-purpose registers, instruction pointer, stack pointer, flags, segment bases (FS for thread-local storage), and optionally FPU/SIMD state (lazily saved via CR0.TS on x86). It also holds scheduling metadata (priority, vruntime, affinity mask).

questions.question: What is the purpose of work-stealing in thread pools?
options: Preventing idle threads — if a thread's local queue is empty, it steals work from another thread's queue
questions.correctAnswer: Preventing idle threads — if a thread's local queue is empty, it steals work from another thread's queue
questions.explanation: In a work-stealing scheduler, each worker thread has its own deque. New tasks are pushed to the local deque. When a thread finishes its work, it steals from the back of another thread's deque (lock-free via CAS). This balances load dynamically without a central dispatcher and minimizes contention (local push/pop, remote steal).

questions.question: What priority inversion problem can occur with real-time threads and mutexes?
options: The mutex can't be locked by real-time threadsaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltalt
A low-priority thread holding a mutex blocks a high-priority real-time thread — the scheduler can't boost the low-priority thread
Mutexes slow down real-time threads too muchaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltaltalt
questions.correctAnswer: A low-priority thread holding a mutex blocks a high-priority real-time thread — the scheduler can't boost the low-priority thread
questions.explanation: Priority inversion: a low-priority thread holds a mutex that a high-priority real-time thread needs. The low-priority thread can't run because medium-priority threads are running (and the real-time thread is blocked). The real-time thread is effectively stuck at the low thread's priority. Solutions: priority inheritance (the mutex holder temporarily inherits the waiter's priority) or avoiding shared locks between priority levels.

questions.question: Explain the three traps described in the note: the `hardware_concurrency()` trap, the lock contention trap, and the false sharing trap. For each, describe the expected vs. actual performance and the fix.
questions.explanation: 1. hardware_concurrency() trap: spawning 16 threads on an 8-core HT CPU expects 16× speedup but gets ~8-12×. SMT threads share execution units (FPU, memory bandwidth). The OS also has other threads competing. Fix: benchmark to find optimal thread count, consider physical cores only. 2. Lock contention trap: 8 threads contending for a mutex around a 1 ns increment. Expected: 8× throughput. Actual: ~0.2× single-thread (7 threads blocked, constant context switches). Fix: use atomics for counters, partition data, batch work outside critical sections. 3. False sharing trap: two threads updating adjacent ints on the same 64-byte cache line. Expected: near-linear scaling. Actual: 50-100× slower due to MESI ping-pong. Fix: `alignas(64)` to place variables on separate cache lines.

questions.question: Compare green threads (fibers) to OS threads. What makes green threads faster to switch, and what is their fatal limitation?
questions.explanation: Green threads switch cooperatively in user space: save callee-saved registers + swap stack pointer (~10-30 ns). OS threads require a kernel context switch: timer interrupt → save all registers → scheduler → restore registers → IRETQ (~1-5 µs, 100-500× slower). The fatal limitation: if one green thread blocks on I/O (e.g., `read()`), all green threads on that OS thread are stuck — the kernel can't distinguish them. OS threads can block independently because the kernel scheduler runs another thread. Green threads require all I/O to be non-blocking (epoll/IOCP) and CPU work to yield periodically.
